sections.content: A DSC distress alert is the digital equivalent of a Mayday call. It is transmitted on VHF Channel 70 and is received by every DSC-equipped station in range. The alert automatically includes the vessel's MMSI, position (from the connected GPS), the time the position was valid, and the nature of distress. There are two methods to send a distress alert: the rapid method for extreme emergencies, and the full method when time permits a more detailed message.

sections.title: Distress Alerts Are for Grave and Imminent Danger Only
sections.content: A DSC distress alert must only be sent when the vessel or a person is in grave and imminent danger and immediate assistance is required. Sending a false distress alert burdens rescue services and puts lives at risk.

The rapid alert method is designed for the worst-case scenario when every second counts. On most DSC controllers, you lift the protective cover over the distress button and press and hold it for approximately 5 seconds. The equipment then transmits a distress alert containing your MMSI, the last valid GPS position and its timestamp, and the nature of distress set to "Undesignated" (since there was no time to select a specific nature). This method requires no menu navigation at all.

sections.title: Rapid Alert — 5-Second Press
sections.content: Lift the cover, press and hold the distress button for 5 seconds. The controller sends a default distress alert with your MMSI, last GPS position, time, and nature of distress set to Undesignated. No menu interaction is needed.

The full alert method is used when the situation allows a few extra moments to provide more detail. You access the DSC menu, select the Distress category, choose the specific nature of distress from a list, verify that the displayed GPS position and time are correct, and then press the distress button to transmit. This gives receiving stations a clearer picture of the emergency before voice contact is established.
The ITU defines the following nature-of-distress codes that can be selected during a full alert. Each code tells rescue coordinators what type of emergency the vessel faces.

rows: Default when no specific nature is selected (rapid alert)
Vessel has been involved in a collision
Vessel is capsizing or listing dangerously
Vessel has lost propulsion or steering and is adrift
Vessel is under attack by pirates or armed robbers

sections.content: Once transmitted, the DSC controller automatically repeats the distress alert at random intervals between 3.5 and 4.5 minutes. This repetition continues until the alert is acknowledged by a coast station via a DSC distress acknowledgement on Channel 70. Individual ships must NOT send a DSC distress acknowledgement — they should listen on Channel 16 for the voice MAYDAY and acknowledge by voice if appropriate. The randomised repeat interval prevents two vessels in distress from continuously colliding on the same time slot.

sections.title: GPS Position Is Automatic
sections.content: When a GPS receiver is connected to the DSC controller, the vessel's position and its timestamp are automatically included in every distress alert. If the GPS is not functioning, the controller will transmit the last stored position, and the timestamp will indicate how old that position is.

sections.prompt: How long must you press and hold the distress button for a rapid DSC distress alert?
sections.explanation: The rapid distress alert is sent by pressing and holding the distress button for approximately 5 seconds. This sends a default alert with the nature of distress set to Undesignated.

sections.prompt: An unacknowledged DSC distress alert automatically repeats every ___ to 4.5 minutes.
sections.explanation: The DSC controller repeats the distress alert at randomised intervals between 3.5 and 4.5 minutes until a distress acknowledgement is received on Channel 70.

sections.prompt: What nature of distress is transmitted when using the rapid (one-button) distress alert method?
sections.explanation: The rapid method does not allow you to select a specific nature, so the controller defaults to Undesignated. To specify the exact nature of distress, use the full alert method via the DSC menu.

points: Two methods exist for sending a DSC distress alert: rapid (5-second button press, Undesignated nature) and full (menu-based, specific nature selected).
A distress alert automatically includes your MMSI, GPS position, position timestamp, and nature of distress.
Eleven nature-of-distress codes are defined, from Undesignated (default) to Man Overboard.
The alert auto-repeats every 3.5-4.5 minutes until acknowledged on Channel 70.
GPS position is embedded automatically when a receiver is connected to the DSC controller.
